segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the PBS Digital Studios logo, accompanied by a distinct, ethereal electronic chime. This transitions to a breathtaking view of Earth from space, set to a gentle, inquisitive orchestral background score. A man in a denim shirt and beige pants then appears against a dark textured background, speaking expressively about the 4-billion-year tale of life on Earth and the difficulty humans have in comprehending such vast stretches of time. As he narrates, scenes of a tawny lioness walking through a savanna and a large pterosaur flying over a lush forest are interspersed. The man continues, explaining how scientists use rocks to understand life's history, which is visually reinforced by a layered rock formation and an animated underwater scene featuring ancient marine creatures like a trilobite and a nautiloid. The background music swells to a grander, more prominent orchestral sound as a geological timeline appears, followed by the word 'EONS' overlaid on a misty prehistoric landscape with dinosaurs. The man reappears, gesturing as he explains the geologic timescale, first with an inset image of layered sedimentary rock, then with an image of Earth from space, continuing his articulate and engaging explanation of Earth's ancient history.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Denim Shirt, speaking expressively against a dark textured background, discussing what actually happened in deep time and in what order. He introduces Danish scientist Nicolas Steno, whose portrait in clerical attire and a book cover titled 'NICOLAI STENONIS DE SOLIDO INTRA SOLIDUM NATURALITER CONTENTO DISSERTATIONIS PRODROMUS' appear to his right, noting Steno published the first laws of stratigraphy—the science of interpreting the strata or layers of rock in Earth's outer surface. A close-up of a Layered Rock Formation, with a vertical white line and text labels 'NEWER' at the top and 'OLDER' at the bottom, visually illustrates Steno's principle that layers closer to the surface must be younger than the layers below them. The speaker returns, gesturing with open hands, emphasizing that this was a pretty revolutionary idea in Steno's day. He then introduces Italian geologist Giovanni Arduino, whose black-and-white bust image appears, explaining that Arduino went a step further by naming the layers of rock. A photograph of snow-capped mountains appears as the speaker mentions that in the 1760s, Arduino studied the Italian Alps, organizing their layers based on their depth and composition. This is followed by a historical sketch of a geological cross-section with handwritten annotations, overlaid with the red text labels 'PRIMARY,' 'SECONDARY,' 'TERTIARY,' and 'QUATERNARY,' representing Arduino's classification of the lowest metamorphic and volcanic rocks, hard sedimentary rocks, and softer alluvial deposits. A series of real rock formations—first a rugged, layered cliff, then a desert rock face with visible strata, and finally a close-up of a sedimentary rock layer—are shown as the speaker explains that because rock layers don't appear in the same order all over the world, there was no way for geologists to compare rocks from one location to another, meaning there could be no universal time scale. The speaker reappears, gesturing with his hands, before introducing English geologist William Smith, whose portrait appears to his right.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Denim Shirt, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, explaining how William Smith "figured out the solution to this problem: fossils." As he elaborates, a framed illustration of various fossilized shells and marine organisms appears to his side, followed by a vertical stack of three fossil images—trilobites, ammonites, and another trilobite—as he details how Smith "realized that fossils of many early species of trilobites are found below ammonite fossils." The scene then transitions to close-up images of ancient rocks: a 4.03 billion-year-old gneiss sample from Canada, and a 3.8 billion-year-old supracrustal rock from Greenland, both labeled with age and credits, as the speaker notes that scientists can "look for signs of the very earliest major geologic events." The speaker reappears, now with historical portraits of Nicolas Steno, Giovanni Arduino, and William Smith displayed to his right, labeled with their names, as he credits these "early geologists" for the "Geologic Time Scale" (GTS). The text 'GEOLOGIC TIME SCALE' appears below the portraits. The video then shifts to a full-screen animated Geological Timeline, showing color-coded horizontal bars for 'EONS', 'ERAS', 'PERIODS', 'EPOCHS', and 'AGES', progressing from 4.6 billion years ago to the present, as the speaker explains the GTS "has been reworked many times" and "is organized into five subgroups." The speaker returns, gesturing as he continues to explain the utility of organizing time in increments, with the Geological Timeline now shown in a smaller inset to his right.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Denim Shirt, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, discussing what life might have looked like in the past, as a white-bordered inset to his right displays a color-coded geological timeline labeled 'EONS' and 'ERAS'. He explains that while they'll take a top-level view, smaller increments like periods and epochs allow for more specific questions about climate and life adaptation. The scene transitions to a full-screen version of the detailed geological timeline, now showing 'PERIODS' and 'EPOCHS' with a finer color gradient, as the speaker continues, stating they will explore the biggest picture of Earth's history by touring all the eons and eras. He defines eons as the largest slices of time, ranging from a half billion to nearly 2 billion years long. A dramatic animated scene labeled 'EONS' then appears, depicting a prehistoric underwater environment with ancient marine life like trilobites and nautiloids, accompanied by a subtle, informative background music. This transitions to a dark, atmospheric title card for the 'HADEAN EON' (4.6–4 billion years ago), set against a blurred Earth from space, as the speaker introduces it as the earliest eon, beginning with Earth's formation around 4.6 billion years ago and ending 4 billion years ago. The speaker reappears full-body, with a vertical timeline on the left listing the Hadean, Archean, Proterozoic, and Paleozoic eras, explaining that the Hadean is unique for having no fossils because the world back then was "just hell," named after the Greek underworld Hades. The video then shows a series of dramatic, white-bordered visualizations illustrating the chaotic conditions of the early Earth: a molten Earth with glowing lava cracks, a fiery volcanic eruption, and a stormy sky with lightning, as the speaker describes the planet being "racked by volcanic activity, cosmic bombardments, raging storms and temperatures that were at times hot enough to melt rock." The Man in Denim Shirt returns, gesturing with open hands to emphasize the immense scale and harshness of the Hadean Eon, noting that even in this "searing wasteland," life may have been able to begin.

segments.integrated_caption: The Man in Denim Shirt, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, begins by discussing the Hadean Eon. He explains that while no fossils have been found, organic carbon in Hadean rocks (illustrated by an inset of a red, layered desert rock formation labeled 'HADEAN ROCK COLLECTION SITE') suggests the earliest life. He describes these first organisms as tiny and single-celled, yet capable of shaping the planet's future, marking a major benchmark. The Hadean Eon concluded with the Earth's crust cooling (visualized by a framed image of a molten Earth with glowing red lava cracks and blue oceans), preparing for continent formation. This cooling initiated the Archean Eon, spanning 4 to 2.5 billion years ago, as a full-screen title card for the 'ARCHEAN EON' appears against a dark, starry background with a timeline. The Man in Denim Shirt returns, explaining the Archean Eon's name derives from the Greek word for origin, and clarifies it was when life truly flourished, forming microbial mats in primordial seas. The scene cuts to a wide animated prehistoric landscape featuring volcanic mountains and a shallow sea filled with stromatolite-like structures. He states the fossils left by these microbes are called stromatolites or stromatoliths, illustrated by a close-up of layered rock labeled 'STROMATOLITE FOSSILS IN GLACIER NATIONAL PARK,' followed by an image of stromatolites in Western Australia, which he notes are among the oldest, dating from the Archean. The speaker returns, gesturing, explaining that during this time, the atmosphere was primarily carbon dioxide, but the emergence of cyanobacteria (shown in an inset of glowing green microbial colonies) was poised to transform it. Then, 2.5 billion years ago, the Archean transitioned into the Proterozoic Eon, meaning 'earlier life,' as a full-screen title card for the 'PROTEROZOIC EON' appears against a deep green underwater scene with light rays. The timeline remains visible on the left as the scene fades into the underwater environment, emphasizing the era's aquatic evolution, while the speaker mentions the emergence of photosynthetic bacteria and multicellular life around this time.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with the Man in Denim Shirt standing against a dark textured background, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He begins by discussing the Hadean Eon, and an inset image appears to his right labeled 'HADEAN ROCK COLLECTION SITE,' showing a red, layered desert rock formation with sparse vegetation, credited to R. Buick / Univ. of Washington. As he continues, the inset changes to a framed image of a molten Earth with glowing red lava cracks and blue oceans, credited to Simone Marchi, illustrating the planet's early chaotic state. The scene transitions to a full-screen title card for the 'ARCHEAN EON' (4–2.5 billion years ago), set against a dark, starry background with a timeline on the left. The Man in Denim Shirt reappears, and the video cuts to a wide animated prehistoric landscape with volcanic mountains and a shallow sea filled with stromatolite-like structures. This is followed by a close-up of layered rock labeled 'STROMATOLITE FOSSILS IN GLACIER NATIONAL PARK,' credited to James St. John, then another image of stromatolites in Western Australia, credited to Ruth Ellison. The speaker returns, gesturing as he explains, and an inset of glowing green microbial colonies appears. The video then transitions to a full-screen title card for the 'PROTEROZOIC EON' (2.5 billion years ago – 541 million years ago), set against a deep green underwater scene with light rays penetrating the water, suggesting ancient marine life. The timeline remains visible on the left, and the scene fades into the underwater environment, emphasizing the era's aquatic evolution.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an animated depiction of eukaryotic cells dividing in a blue, ethereal environment, labeled 'EUKARYOTES', accompanied by a consistent, light instrumental background music. The Man in Denim Shirt, speaking with a clear, informative tone, explains how increased oxygen led to the development of "ancestral eukaryotes," describing them as cells with a nucleus and organelles wrapped in membranes. He continues, stating that eukaryotes evolved into "really big, complex, and sometimes kind of weird forms of life," which are visually represented by fossil images of 'CHARNIA', described as frond-like, and 'DICKINSONIA', described as plate-shaped. The Man in Denim Shirt then states these larger organisms diversified, leading to the "next and current eon, the Phanerozoic," as a timeline graphic highlights the 'PHANEROZOIC EON' (541 million years ago – present). The full geological timeline expands, and the speaker explains that the Phanerozoic's name means "visible life," becoming home to "trees, dinosaurs, newts, ardvarks, us humans, basically life as we know it," illustrated by scenes of lush ferns, a prehistoric landscape with dinosaurs, and a modern cityscape. The Man in Denim Shirt reappears, humorously checking on the viewer, noting they've covered "three and a half billion years already" and are moving to explore the Phanerozoic Eon through its "eras," the next level down in the divisions of time, as the timeline is displayed again. A dramatic prehistoric scene with dinosaurs is then overlaid with the word 'ERAS', followed by a title card for the 'PALEOZOIC ERA' (541–252 million years ago) against a blurred prehistoric background with the geological timeline visible, as the speaker introduces it as "the first era of our current eon."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with an animated depiction of eukaryotic cells in a blue, ethereal environment, showing a single cell dividing into two, with the label 'EUKARYOTES' displayed in the lower-left corner. The Man in Denim Shirt reappears, gesturing with both hands raised, as the timeline is displayed again. The video then shows a dramatic prehistoric scene with dinosaurs, overlaid with the word 'ERAS' in large white letters. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a title card for the 'Paleozoic Era' (541–252 million years ago), displayed over a dark, textured background with a geological timeline on the left. A man in a denim shirt, speaking clearly and expressively against a fossil-etched backdrop, introduces this era as beginning 541 million years ago, defined by the diversification of visible life, which started with a "bang," an "explosion"—the Cambrian explosion. As he speaks, the word 'explosion' appears in glowing purple text, followed by an animated underwater scene depicting prehistoric marine life, including a large, segmented creature with claws and spiny organisms, swimming over a coral-like seabed. The scene then cuts to a 'Proterozoic Eon' title card (2.5 billion years ago – 541 million years ago) set against a deep green underwater environment with light rays. The speaker reappears, explaining that the rapid growth in diversity and complexity in the world's oceans was so significant that all prior eons—the Hadean, Archean, and Proterozoic (listed on a timeline to his right)—are collectively known as the Precambrian. He continues, detailing how the Paleozoic's start saw the fossil record suddenly reveal complex animals with "mineralized remains," or "hard parts" like shells and exoskeletons. Another animated underwater environment then shows trilobite-like creatures and sponges, with a fossil image of a trilobite appearing in a white-bordered inset, as the speaker identifies trilobites as the first truly widespread new animals, used as index fossils for centuries. The animation continues with more diverse marine life, including a nautiloid and a shark-like creature, swimming among colorful coral and tube worms, as he describes fish developing teeth and jaws, dominating the seas, including the first sharks and armored giants. The speaker returns, now with two inset images to his right: a fossilized trilobite and a green, segmented 'COOKSONIA IMAGE BY SMITH609', explaining that the land, previously barren, was finally being populated by plants and then arthropods by 370 million years ago, before the audio fades. Throughout, a calm, slightly dramatic background music accompanies the speaker's clear, informative narration.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of fossilized footprints in cracked, reddish-brown sedimentary rock, labeled 'FOSSILIZED FOOTPRINTS', as the Man in Denim Shirt narrates how early amphibians evolved and left these "first vertebrate footprints in the mud." The scene transitions to a view of Earth from space, illustrating the supercontinent Pangea with an enormous desert at its center, which the Man in Denim Shirt explains was populated by ancestors of reptiles and mammals. He appears, speaking with expressive hand gestures, as three illustrations of prehistoric reptiles by Nobu Tamura appear beside him, representing these thriving creatures. An animated Dimetrodon with a vibrant sail is then shown, followed by an underwater scene of trilobites swimming among coral-like formations, which the Man in Denim Shirt refers to as "our friends the trilobites" who survived previous extinctions. A gentle, melancholic synth sound plays as he adds, "I still miss those guys." The Man in Denim Shirt reappears, and the bold white text 'THE GREAT DYING' appears beside him, as he describes this event as "the most severe extinction in our planet's history," with its exact cause still unclear. A map highlights the Falkland Islands (Islas Malvinas) in the Scotia Sea, presented as a possible site for "a possible meteorite impact off the coast of South America," followed by an aerial view of the Siberian Traps, illustrating "massive volcanic eruptions" that covered Pangea in lava. The Man in Denim Shirt returns, explaining that both incidents coincided with the end of the Paleozoic era, as a vertical geological timeline appears to his left, listing the Hadean, Archean, Proterozoic, Paleozoic, Mesozoic, and Cenozoic eons, emphasizing the vastness of geological time and the multiple causes of the extinction.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Denim Shirt, wearing a dark denim shirt, speaking expressively against a dark textured background, explaining how a past chapter of life 'ended in nearly absolute death.' A vertical geological timeline, detailing eons and eras from Hadean to Phanerozoic, appears on the left. He continues, noting that 'it took millions of years for life to recover,' leading to 'a new world, the Mesozoic Era,' as a dark, smoky title card for 'MESOZOIC ERA 252 - 66 million years ago' appears. The Man in Denim Shirt describes this as 'the age of reptiles,' highlighting their success and 'most famous forms, like dinosaurs, pterosaurs, and a variety of marine species.' This is visually supported by animated scenes of a large sauropod dinosaur on a beach, a pterosaur flying over a green landscape, and a dynamic T. rex. He further explains that 'all of the non-avian dinosaurs lived only in the Mesozoic,' making them 'one of the best index fossils,' and that 'many modern groups of organisms also evolved in the shadows of the reptiles, like mammals, frogs, bees, and flowering plants.' A museum display of a T. rex skeleton is shown, then the Man in Denim Shirt reappears, gesturing towards a vertical stack of four small illustrations on the right: a shrew-like mammal, a green frog, a bee, and yellow flowers. He then states that 'the Mesozoic Era came to an end 66 million years ago with yet another episode of devastation,' known as 'the Cretaceous-Paleogene or KPG extinction event.' The geological timeline reappears before a bold title card announces the 'CRETACEOUS-PALEOGENE EXTINCTION EVENT.' The Man in Denim Shirt elaborates that 'the biggest' cause was 'a giant asteroid that struck the Earth,' sending 'enormous amounts of ash into the atmosphere, blocking out sunlight, and creating a vicious cold snap.' An animated scene of a massive, fiery asteroid streaking towards Earth illustrates this. He concludes that 'entire plant communities died, and the animals that relied on those plants perished with them,' as the video shows a wide shot of a desert rock formation labeled 'CRETACEOUS-PALEOGENE BOUNDARY,' featuring layered sedimentary rock and sparse vegetation under a cloudy sky. Throughout, a subtle, dramatic background music accompanies his clear, informative speech.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dramatic animated asteroid descending through Earth's atmosphere, trailing fire and smoke, as the Man in Denim Shirt explains that an iridium layer found at the end of the Mesozoic, rare on Earth but common in asteroids, points to an impact. This is visually reinforced by a map highlighting the Chicxulub Crater in the Yucatán Peninsula, which he identifies as the 'smoking gun' for the asteroid hypothesis. Animated prehistoric creatures, including a pterosaur gliding, a Stegosaurus walking, and a long-necked sauropod, illustrate his description of the KPG extinction's victims: the biggest reptiles of land, sea, and sky, including non-avian dinosaurs, while birds survived. The Man then appears, stating, "Okay, we have 66 million years to go," marking the last major extinction. In a lighthearted moment, he offers "pre-moistened towellettes" with the sound of a packet tearing and rustling, humorously suggesting they wipe 'iridium' from their foreheads. He then explains that with the great reptiles gone, smaller animals thrived in the next era, the Cenozoic. A vertical geological timeline appears, followed by a full-screen 'CENOZOIC ERA 66 million years ago - present' title card, as he declares, "This is our era," marking the rise of mammals. A lush jungle scene then visually represents the climate warming and jungles stretching across the planet, before a brief dark underwater scene transitions back to the Man, who concludes that mammals quickly recovered in this 'hot house world'.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a dramatic animated scene of a massive asteroid glowing as it descends through Earth's atmosphere, trailing fire and smoke, set against a backdrop of clouds and stars, visually representing the Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event. This transitions to a map highlighting the Yucatán Peninsula with a red circle marking the Chicxulub Crater, emphasizing the impact site. The scene shifts to animated prehistoric creatures: a pterosaur gliding over a forest, followed by a Stegosaurus walking through a misty landscape, and then a long-necked sauropod moving across a plain with mountains in the background. The video then cuts to the Man in Denim Shirt standing against a dark textured background, speaking expressively with hand gestures. To his right, an inset image of colorful birds in flight appears briefly. As he continues, a vertical geological timeline appears on the left, listing eons and eras from the Hadean to the Phanerozoic, with time spans. He gestures toward the timeline, emphasizing the Cenozoic Era, which is then highlighted with a full-screen title card reading 'CENOZOIC ERA 66 million years ago - present' over a smoky, dark background. The scene transitions to a lush, vibrant jungle with a stream and dense foliage, then to a dark, underwater prehistoric scene with light rays penetrating the water, before returning to the Man in Denim Shirt, who continues his explanation with animated hand movements, maintaining eye contact with the camera.

segments.integrated_caption: The Man in Denim Shirt, speaking expressively against a dark background, introduces the emergence of mammal groups like whales, bats, rodents, and primates, as corresponding framed illustrations appear to his right. As he describes ice caps growing and the planet's water being taken up, the scene transitions to a full-screen view of Earth from space, centered on Antarctica. He then explains how drier conditions created grasslands, where ancestral horses like the illustrated Mesohippus (credited to Heinrich Harder) were hunted by early predators. The speaker reappears, now with a framed fossilized skull of 'SAHELANTHROPUS' to his right, and a stylized Earth model to his left, as he details how this species became the first known primate to walk upright 7 million years ago. He continues, explaining that 2.6 million years ago, ice caps expanded further, leading to the Ice Age, where modern life forms, including giants like the vividly illustrated woolly mammoth, thrived alongside ground sloths and saber-tooth cats. The video then shows a lioness walking through a dry savanna, followed by a prehistoric human running with hyenas in a dusty plain, as the speaker discusses the end of this era of diversity and the extinction of Ice Age megafauna around 11,700 years ago, questioning humanity's role. Throughout, a consistent, slightly dramatic background music plays.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with the Man in Denim Shirt standing against a dark textured background, speaking expressively with his hands clasped or gesturing. As he speaks, a series of framed illustrations appear to his right: a blue whale, a small rodent, a bat, and a hominid, suggesting a discussion on diverse species. The scene transitions to a full-screen view of Earth from space, centered on Antarctica, emphasizing the planet’s icy continent. This shifts to a framed illustration of a prehistoric grassland with a herd of Mesohippus, labeled with the artist’s name, Heinrich Harder. The speaker reappears, now with a framed image of a fossilized skull labeled 'SAHELANTHROPUS' to his right, accompanied by the text '***PROBABLY? EXPERTS DISAGREE ABOUT THE FIRST BIPEDAL PRIMATE.' A small, stylized Earth model appears to his left, showing green landmasses and blue oceans. The video then cuts to a vivid illustration of a woolly mammoth in a snowy landscape with flying birds and distant mountains. This is followed by a lioness walking through a dry savanna, then a scene of a prehistoric human running alongside hyenas in a dusty, grassy plain. The speaker returns, continuing his narration with hands clasped, maintaining the same dark background throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The Man in Denim Shirt, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures against a dark, textured background, articulates that "our species has shaped the earth to its will," emphasizing humanity's "huge impact on habitats, other organisms, and the biosphere itself." He explains that "it's the most dominant forms of life that define each phase of deep time," concluding that despite humanity's brief existence, "we are the authors of the next chapter." He speculates that "the epoch of humans may be detected by the marks we make on the land, the traces of our cities and farms," and that "our very bodies will be the index fossils of this time," before stating, "No matter how our chapter ends, we get to be characters in a truly amazing story." Upbeat, adventurous background music with a driving beat and synth elements then begins as the video transitions through a dramatic animated T. rex in a misty, prehistoric setting with sparks, followed by a real-world logging operation where a red forklift moves logs in a mountainous area. This is succeeded by an aerial view of a modern cityscape at night, showcasing light trails from vehicles on a highway, before cutting to a green tractor plowing a field under a cloudy sky. The Man in Denim Shirt reappears, now with a vertical geological timeline graphic on the left side of the screen listing eons and eras, thanking viewers for joining him on this "epic or epoch journey through geologic time." He then asks, "Now, what do you want to know about the story of life on Earth?" The scene transitions to a deep blue underwater animated background with the word 'EONS' in large white letters, featuring swimming fish and a shark, as the Man in Denim Shirt encourages viewers to "Let us know in the comments, and don't forget to go to youtube.com/eons and subscribe." The text 'YOUTUBE.COM/EONS' appears on screen. He adds that "the fun doesn't end here," advising viewers to "check out some of our sister channels from PBS Digital Studios," as a small inset appears in the top left corner showing a woman with dark hair, labeled 'BRAINCRAFT', toward which he gestures. The video concludes with a full-screen credits sequence over the underwater background, listing production roles, image sources, and social media handles for the EONS show, with the PBS Digital Studios logo visible in the top left corner, as the adventurous background music continues to play and then fades out.
